steps.title: Numbers: System 1 of 2
steps.desc: Korean has TWO number systems. This is Sino-Korean — the one from Chinese. You need this one for dates, money, and phone numbers. The other system (native Korean) comes next lesson.
goals: Know when Sino-Korean numbers are used

steps.note:
SINO-KOREAN numbers from Chinese.

Use for: dates, money, phone numbers,
minutes, floors, addresses.

NOT for counting objects (that's native Korean).

사, 오, 육.

⚠️ 사 also means 'death' in Chinese.
This is why 4 is unlucky in Korea.
Many Korean buildings skip the 4th floor
or label it 'F' instead.

칠, 팔, 구, 십.

십 is the base for all compound numbers.
십일 = 10+1 = 11.
이십 = 2×10 = 20.

steps.title: 🧮 Number Compounds = Pure Math
steps.text:
Korean numbers are LEGO math:

십일 = 십(10) + 일(1) = 11
십이 = 십(10) + 이(2) = 12
이십 = 이(2) × 십(10) = 20
삼십오 = 삼(3)×십(10) + 오(5) = 35

Digits 1-9 + 십 → teens
Digit × 십 + digit → any number up to 99

No new words needed after 일-십.
Pure arithmetic.

steps.q: Why do many Korean buildings skip the 4th floor?

steps.hint: 010 format: most Korean mobile numbers start 010
Dates, money, phone numbers, minutes — all Sino-Korean

steps.title: Bigger Numbers
steps.desc: Hundreds, thousands, and ten-thousands. The key twist: Korean counts by TEN-THOUSANDS, not thousands. This trips up English speakers — but once you see it, it's logical.

steps.note:
백: hundred.
이백: 200, 삼백: 300, 오백: 500.
구백구십구: 999.

1백 is just 백 (not 일백).

천: thousand.
2천 = 이천, 3천 = 삼천.
1천 = 천 (not 일천).

A typical Korean lunch: 오천~팔천 원 (₩5,000-8,000).

만: TEN THOUSAND (not just a thousand).

⚠️ Key difference:
English groups by thousands (1,000; 10,000; 100,000)
Korean groups by TEN-THOUSANDS

10,000 = 일만
100,000 = 십만 (ten ten-thousands)
1,000,000 = 백만 (hundred ten-thousands)

This is why Korean prices look huge to English speakers.

COMPOUND: 전화 (phone call) + 번호 (number).

공 = zero for phone numbers (not 영)
010-1234-5678: 공일공 일이삼사 오육칠팔

전화하다: to phone someone (전화 + 하다)

steps.title: 💡 Why Korean Prices Look Enormous
steps.text:
You see a price tag: 25,000원

English brain: 'Twenty-five thousand won'
Korean brain: 이만오천 원 (2 ten-thousands + 5 thousands)

Korea groups by 만 (10,000), not 천 (1,000).

Practical examples:
₩1,000: 천 원 (1 thousand)
₩10,000: 만 원 (1 ten-thousand) — largest common coin
₩50,000: 오만 원 (5 ten-thousands) — largest bill, has King Sejong
₩100,000: 십만 원 (10 ten-thousands)

만 원 is your basic mental unit for Korean money.

steps.hint: 오(5) × 만(10,000) = 50,000 — the biggest Korean bill
만 = 10,000 is the base grouping unit

steps.q: 전화번호 = 전화 + 번호. What does 전화 mean?

steps.hint: Phone numbers use 공 for zero

steps.title: Numbers: System 2 of 2
steps.desc: Native Korean numbers. These are the ones you use for counting actual things, telling hours, and stating age. They look nothing like Sino-Korean — but you only need 1-99.
goals: Know the 4 number contractions (한, 두, 세, 네)
Apply the right system in context

steps.note:
NATIVE KOREAN numbers.

Use for:
• Counting objects (with counters)
• Hours: 세 시 (3 o'clock)
• Age: 스물 한 살 (21 years old)

NOT for dates, money, phone numbers.

여섯~열: the harder ones to memorize.

Good news: native numbers are mainly used 1-99.
Above 100, Sino-Korean takes over completely.
Focus on 1-20 first. The rest follows.

steps.example: 사과 세 개 / 커피 두 잔
steps.note:
CONTRACTIONS before counters:
하나→한, 둘→두, 셋→세, 넷→네

Only THESE FOUR contract. 다섯~열 stay the same.
하나 개 ✗ / 한 개 ✓
둘 잔 ✗ / 두 잔 ✓
다섯 명 ✓ (no contraction needed for 5+)

steps.title: 📊 Two Systems: The Cheat Sheet
steps.text:
SINO-KOREAN (일, 이, 삼...):
• Dates: 삼월 십오일 (March 15th)
• Money: 이천 원 (₩2,000)
• Phone numbers: 공일공...
• Minutes: 삼십 분 (30 min)
• Floors: 오 층 (5th floor)

NATIVE KOREAN (하나, 둘, 셋...):
• Counting things: 사과 세 개 (3 apples)
• Hours: 세 시 (3 o'clock)
• Age: 스물 살 (20 years old)

Tip: if you're unsure, listen to how Koreans say it.
After 100 encounters, your ear knows which to use.

steps.hint: Hours use NATIVE numbers, and 셋 contracts to 세 before counters
Dates always use Sino-Korean: 삼월 십오일

steps.q: Which four native numbers contract before counters?
steps.hint: Only the first four: 하나→한, 둘→두, 셋→세, 넷→네
다섯 (5) does NOT contract. No change needed.

steps.s: 커피 ___ 잔 주세요. (2 cups of coffee)
steps.hint: 둘 contracts to 두 before counters
셋 contracts to 세 before counters
3:30 — native for hours, Sino-Korean for minutes
하나 contracts to 한 before counters: 한 명, not 하나 명

steps.title: Korean Counts by Category
steps.desc: In Korean, you can't just say 'three apples'. You need a COUNTER word. Think of English 'three CUPS of coffee' — Korean does this for EVERYTHING. Different categories, different counters.

steps.note:
개: general counter for THINGS and OBJECTS.

Pattern: noun + number + counter.
사과 세 개: apple three-개.

Numbers 1-4 contract:
한 개 (1), 두 개 (2), 세 개 (3), 네 개 (4)
다섯 개 (5), 여섯 개 (6) — no contraction needed.

명: general counter for PEOPLE.
두 명: 2 people. 세 명: 3 people.

분 is the POLITE version of 명:
두 분이세요? (Are there two of you?) — in restaurants.
선생님 두 분 (two teachers — honorific).

잔: counter for cups, glasses, drinks.
한 잔: one cup. 두 잔: two cups.

커피 한 잔 주세요 is THE coffee order phrase.
Like English 'two cups of'— but always required.

마리: counter for ANIMALS (all animals).
고양이 한 마리: one cat.
강아지 두 마리: two puppies.
물고기 세 마리: three fish.

All animals use 마리, regardless of size.

steps.title: 🗂️ Why Counters? Korean Classifies the World
steps.text:
Korean sorts things into categories and counts each differently:

개 — general objects
명 / 분 — people (plain / honorific)
잔 — cups, glasses
마리 — animals
권 — books, notebooks
장 — flat things (paper, tickets, photos)
대 — machines, vehicles
벌 — clothing items

You don't need all of these now.
Just know: EVERY noun has a counter category.
The most common ones come naturally through use.

Think of it like English 'sheets of paper' or 'heads of cattle' — but systematically applied to everything.

steps.q: The POLITE counter for people is:
steps.hint: 분 is the honorific version of 명
Animals use 마리. 5 = 다섯 (no contraction needed)
하나 contracts to 한 before counters
Polite counter for people = 분, 2 = 두

steps.s: 책 세 ___ 주세요. (3 books — use the right counter)

steps.q: Which counter would you use for 'two tickets'?
steps.hint: 장 = flat things like paper, tickets, photos

steps.title: Telling Time
steps.desc: Hours use NATIVE numbers. Minutes use SINO-Korean. Yes, both systems in one sentence. You've already learned both — now you put them together.

steps.note:
몇: how many. 시: o'clock (counter for hours).
지금 몇 시예요?: 'What time is it now?'

시 uses NATIVE Korean numbers:
한 시 (1:00), 두 시 (2:00), 세 시 (3:00)...

분: minutes (counter for minutes).
분 uses SINO-KOREAN numbers:
십 분 (10 min), 이십 분 (20 min), 삼십 분 (30 min)

반: half — 세 시 반 = 3:30 (very common shortcut).
세 시 반 = 세 시 삼십 분 (same meaning)

SINO-KOREAN COMPOUNDS:
오 (noon/午) + 전 (before/前): before noon = AM
오 (noon/午) + 후 (after/後): after noon = PM

전 and 후 are reusable morphemes:
식전 (before eating), 식후 (after eating)
전날 (day before), 다음날 (day after)

반: half.
두 시 반: 2:30 (two-thirty).
세 시 반: 3:30.

Used instead of '삼십 분' in conversation.
두 시 반 = 두 시 삼십 분 (same meaning, 반 is faster).

steps.hint: Hours use NATIVE Korean. 셋 → 세 before counter
10 o'clock: hours use NATIVE Korean. 10 = 열

steps.q: 전 in 오전 also appears in 식전 (before eating). 전 means:
steps.hint: 오전 = before noon; 식전 = before a meal
두 시 반 not 이 시 반: hours always use NATIVE numbers

steps.title: How Much?
steps.desc: Shopping survival. Korean money uses Sino-Korean numbers and the won (원). You already know the numbers — now apply them to prices.

steps.note:
THE survival shopping question.
이것은 얼마예요?: How much is this?
저것은 얼마예요?: How much is that?

얼마: how much. 예요: is.

이거 얼마예요? (shorter, casual version) is also common.

원: Korean won (₩).

Common prices:
₩1,000 천 원: cheap snack
₩5,000 오천 원: cheap lunch item
₩10,000 만 원: mid-range
₩50,000 오만 원: largest bill (King Sejong's face)

거스름돈: change (money returned).

영수증 주세요: Receipt, please.
카드로 해요: I'll pay by card.
현금으로 해요: I'll pay cash.

SINO-KOREAN: 할 (cut/reduce) + 인 (price).
할인 있어요?: 'Is there a discount?'

세일: sale (Konglish from English 'sale')
이십 퍼센트 할인: 20% off
퍼센트: percent (Konglish)

steps.title: 💳 Paying in Korea
steps.text:
Korea is largely cashless:

카드 (card): credit/debit cards everywhere
현금 (cash): still needed at small restaurants
모바일 결제: mobile payment (very common — Kakao Pay, Naver Pay)

Useful phrases:
카드로 계산해도 돼요?
→ Can I pay by card?

영수증 주세요.
→ Receipt, please.

잔돈 없어요.
→ I don't have exact change.

steps.hint: 오만 원 = the largest bill. Sejong invented Hangul.

steps.q: 'Can I pay by card?' in Korean:

steps.s: A: 이거 {1}예요? B: 팔천 원이에요. A: {2} 주세요.

steps.title: P26 Moment: You've Been Doing This Since Day One
steps.desc: You've seen this pattern in every single sentence of this course. You've USED it naturally for 2 full units. Today it gets its name: Korean is SOV. Subject. Object. Verb. The verb always goes last.
goals: Verify it in sentences you already know

steps.exampleEn: I drink coffee. (lit: I coffee drink)
steps.note:
Korean is SOV:
S: Subject (저는 — I)
O: Object (커피를 — coffee)
V: Verb LAST (마셔요 — drink)

English is SVO: I DRINK coffee.
Korean is SOV: I coffee DRINK.

You've been doing this naturally since lesson 1.
Now you know what it's called.

steps.exampleEn: I am a student. / There is coffee. / I don't know.
steps.note:
Check every sentence you know:

저는 학생이에요 → S + V (no object)
커피가 있어요 → S + V (있다 = exist)
저는 밥을 먹어요 → S + O + V ✓
저는 커피를 마셔요 → S + O + V ✓

Verb. Always. Last.

steps.exampleEn: Yesterday I ate lunch at school.
steps.note:
In a longer sentence:
어제 (yesterday) — time
저는 (I) — subject
학교에서 (at school) — location
점심을 (lunch) — object
먹었어요 (ate) — VERB LAST

The middle elements can shift.
The verb CANNOT move from the end.

English: I ate lunch at school yesterday.
Korean: Yesterday I at-school lunch ATE.

steps.title: ⭐ Why SOV Feels Natural After Two Units
steps.text:
You've been reading Korean as SOV this whole time.
Here's proof — spot the verbs at the END:

저는 학생이에요 → ...이에요 (last)
커피가 있어요 → ...있어요 (last)
여기 있어요? → ...있어요? (last)
화장실이 어디에 있어요? → ...있어요? (last)
저도 몰라요 → ...몰라요 (last)

Your brain already knows SOV.
This lesson just gave your brain a name for it.

Key takeaway: NEVER move the verb.
Even in a sentence with 10 elements,
the verb is the caboose.

steps.q: In Korean, where does the verb go?
steps.hint: SOV = verb at the END, always
The verb is always the final element

steps.q: Which sentence shows correct SOV order?
steps.hint: Subject first, object middle, verb LAST

steps.q: Korean SOV vs English SVO: what's different?
opts: Verb is in the middle in Korean
steps.hint: English: I DRINK coffee. Korean: I coffee DRINK.

steps.s: 저는 밥을 ___. (I eat rice — put the verb last)
steps.hint: Verb = last. 밥을 먹다 = to eat rice
The verb (read) goes last in this SOV sentence
I drink coffee — object comes before verb

steps.q: 어제 저는 학교에서 점심을 먹었어요. The verb is:
Can you move 어제 in the sentence 어제 저는 밥을 먹었어요?
opts: Yes, time words can shift, but the verb stays last
No, nothing can move in Korean
steps.ans: Yes, time words can shift, but the verb stays last
steps.hint: Middle elements are flexible. The verb position is fixed at the end.

lessons.title: ★ 해요체 Named: You've Been Speaking It All Along

steps.title: The Speech Level You're Already Using
steps.desc: Every verb you've spoken ends in -요. That -요 is not random. It's a deliberate social choice. It has a name. And you've been making that choice correctly for 30+ lessons without knowing it.
goals: Understand what a speech level is and why Korean has them
See the 3 practical speech levels (not 7 — be calm)
Hear 합쇼체 and recognise you've already encountered it
Know when 반말 exists but not use it yet

steps.exampleEn: go / eat / study / exist — all polite
steps.note:
해요체 = 해요 + 체(style/register).
Every sentence ending in -요 is 해요체.

Why 해요? Because 하다 → 해요 is its core form.

해요체 is:
✅ Safe with everyone — strangers, colleagues, acquaintances
✅ Not too formal, not too casual
✅ The default for any situation you're unsure about
✅ What 99% of Korean learners use daily, forever

You have been speaking 해요체 from Lesson 1, Unit 1.

steps.title: 🗺️ Why Korean Has Speech Levels At All
steps.text:
Korean encodes your social relationship with the listener DIRECTLY into the verb ending.

This is not about being 'polite' in an English sense.
This is social cartography — your words tell the listener:
• How old you think they are relative to you
• What your relationship is
• How formal the situation is

Getting the speech level wrong is not just rude.
It's grammatically incoherent — like a wrong tense in English.

Koreans ask your age immediately because they NEED to know
which verb endings to use with you.
It's not nosy. It's grammatically necessary.

deepDive.title: The Full System (academic knowledge, not required yet)
deepDive.text:
Korean has 7 historically documented speech levels.
In practice, only 3 matter for daily life:

1. 해요체 (-아/어요) — YOU ARE HERE
   Polite informal. Safe default with everyone.

2. 합쇼체 (-습니다/ㅂ니다) — FORMAL POLITE
   Offices, broadcasts, military, announcements.
   You hear it on planes: '착륙하겠습니다'

3. 반말 (-아/어, no -요) — CASUAL
   Close friends, younger people, children.
   Never with strangers or elders.

The other 4 levels (하오체, 하게체, 해라체, 해체)
are found in historical dramas and literature.
Not needed for modern daily life.

Conclusion: 3 levels. Not 7. You're already in one.

steps.exampleEn: Thank you (formal) / Goodbye (formal) / Nice to meet you (formal)
steps.note:
합쇼체 = the formal, stiff register.
You've already heard it — you just didn't know the name.

감사합니다 (Unit 1) = 하다 → 합니다 form
안녕히 가세요 → formal version: 안녕히 가십시오

FORMATION:
Stem ends in vowel + ㅂ니다: 가다 → 갑니다
Stem ends in consonant + 습니다: 먹다 → 먹습니다

WHEN to use:
• Job interviews
• Meeting clients / senior strangers
• Official broadcasts and announcements
• Military, airlines, formal ceremonies

⚡ You don't need to produce this yet. Just recognise it.

steps.q: You meet a Korean colleague for the first time. Which register?
opts: Either 해요체 or 합쇼체 — both are appropriate
steps.ans: Either 해요체 or 합쇼체 — both are appropriate
steps.hint: Both are polite. 해요체 is warmer; 합쇼체 is more formal. Both are correct.

steps.example: 가다 → 갑니다 / 먹다 → 먹습니다 / 하다 → 합니다
steps.exampleEn: go → go (formal) / eat → eat (formal) / do → do (formal)
steps.note:
PATTERN:
가다: stem 가 (vowel ending) → 가 + ㅂ니다 = 갑니다
먹다: stem 먹 (consonant ending) → 먹 + 습니다 = 먹습니다
하다: stem 하 (vowel ending) → 하 + ㅂ니다 = 합니다

Same stem as 해요체. Just a different 'politeness car' snapped on.
🚂 TRAIN: same engine (가, 먹, 하), different car (-ㅂ니다 vs -아/어요)

steps.title: 🌱 반말 — It Exists. Don't Use It Yet.
steps.text:
반말 = casual speech. No -요.

해요체: 가요 / 먹어요 / 있어요
반말: 가 / 먹어 / 있어

Notice: 반말 is often just the stem + connecting vowel, no -요.

WHEN to use 반말:
• Close friends your age or younger
• Siblings (informal)
• Young children
• NEVER with strangers, elders, colleagues you don't know well

Using 반말 with the wrong person is NOT just rude.
It signals you think you're above them.

⚡ JRTF: We'll learn반말 properly in Unit 10.
For now: it exists, you'll hear it in dramas, don't use it.
Stick to 해요체 for everything.

deepDive.title: Why Koreans Sometimes Sound Rude in Dramas
deepDive.text:
If you've watched Korean dramas, you've heard 반말 constantly.
Close friends, couples, family — everyone uses it.

Drama dialogue = 반말 between intimates.
Real life with strangers = always 해요체 or higher.

Korean learners who learn from dramas often pick up 반말 first.
This is a mistake. It causes social discomfort.

The correct order:
1. 해요체 (you're here now) — master this first
2. 합쇼체 — for formal contexts
3. 반말 — when you have real relationships

Most Korean speakers will correct you kindly.
But they'll think more of you if you use 해요체 correctly
than if you try 반말 too soon.

steps.q: 먹다 in 합쇼체 formal present is:
가다 in 합쇼체 formal present is:

steps.hint: -겠습니다 is 합쇼체 + future mood. Formal register, as expected on a broadcast.

steps.q: Which register have you been speaking since Unit 1, Lesson 1?
steps.hint: Every -요 ending you've used = 해요체. You've been in it from day one.

steps.title: ✅ Where You Stand Right Now
steps.text:
You now know:

🚂 How verbs are built (stem + ending)
⚡ Why -아요 vs -어요 (vowel harmony)
🎙️ What speech level you're speaking (해요체)
🗺️ What the other levels are and when to use them

This is the complete engine of Korean verb formation.
Every verb you encounter from here on — you can analyse:
• What's the stem?
• What vowel does it contain?
• Which ending does it take?
• What speech level is this?

You are no longer memorising chunks.
You are generating Korean.

steps.title: Dates and Birthdays
steps.desc: Dates follow a BIG to SMALL order: year → month → day. The opposite of American English but similar to British and many other languages. All use Sino-Korean numbers.
goals: Decompose 생일 and key date words

steps.note:
DATE FORMAT: Year → Month → Day
(Big to small, opposite of US English)

년: year
월: month (same 월 as 월요일/Monday!)
일: day

All use SINO-KOREAN numbers.

SINO-KOREAN: 생 (birth/生) + 일 (day/日).
'Birth-day': same logic as English!

생: same as 학생!
언제: when (time question word)

생일 축하해요!: Happy birthday!

steps.en: this year / next year / last year
steps.exampleEn: When is your birthday this year?
steps.note:
올해: this year (native Korean)
내년: next year (Sino: 내(next)+년(year))
작년: last year (Sino: 작(past)+년)

내년에 한국에 가고 싶어요.
→ I want to go to Korea next year.

축하: congratulations.
축하해요: 'Congratulations!' / 'Happy [occasion]!'

생일 축하해요: Happy birthday!
결혼 축하해요: Congratulations on your wedding!
졸업 축하해요: Congratulations on graduating!

steps.hint: 생 = birth, same morpheme as 학생 (study-person)
Big to small: year first, day last

steps.title: Monday Through Sunday
steps.desc: Every day name is a compound: [element] + 요일. The elements come from astronomy and Chinese philosophy — the same system as many East Asian languages, and even English!
goals: Ask and say what day it is

steps.exampleEn: I go to school on Monday.
steps.note:
COMPOUND: 월(moon/月) + 요(day-of) + 일(day).
'Moon-day' = Monday.

월: same as 월(month)!
Monday in Korean = Moon-day. Same as English!

화(fire/火) + 요일: Fire-day = Tuesday
수(water/水) + 요일: Water-day = Wednesday
목(wood/木) + 요일: Wood-day = Thursday

These are 3 of the 5 Chinese philosophical elements.
Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday = Fire, Water, Wood.

금(gold/金) + 요일: Gold-day = Friday
토(earth/土) + 요일: Earth-day = Saturday
일(sun/日) + 요일: Sun-day = Sunday

일요일 = Sun-day. Same as English!
5 Elements complete: wood, fire, earth, gold, water
+ sun, moon.

무슨: what kind of / which.
무슨 요일이에요?: 'What day (of the week) is it?'

오늘: today
내일: tomorrow
어제: yesterday

오늘 무슨 요일이에요? is the natural question.

steps.hint: 월 = moon AND month. Monday = Moon-day.

steps.q: Which Korean day name matches 'Sun-day' in English?

steps.s: 월, 화, 수, 목, 금, 토, ___ (complete the week)
___요일에 회의가 있어요. (There's a meeting on Monday)

steps.hint: Today is Wednesday, tomorrow is Thursday

steps.q: The five elements in Korean days come from:

steps.title: Unit 3 Complete
steps.desc: Numbers, counters, time, dates, days, money — and the SOV rule you now fully OWN. Every quiz item uses only Units 1-3 vocabulary.

steps.example: 세 시 반이에요. 오늘은 화요일이에요. 사과 두 개 주세요.
steps.exampleEn: It's 3:30. Today is Tuesday. Two apples, please.
steps.note:
TWO number systems with distinct uses.
COUNTERS: 개/명/잔/마리 + more.
TIME: native hours + Sino minutes.
DATES: Sino-Korean, big→small.
SOV: verb ALWAYS last.

steps.hint: Drinks counter 잔, native number 셋→세
Subject Object Verb — verb at the end, always

steps.s: 저는 책을 ___. (I read books — verb last)
steps.hint: SOV: verb goes at the end
SOV: eating lunch at school — verb last
